Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to sit down and study, but I don't really have a quiet space at home and my resources are limited, so I'd end up doing bits and pieces whenever I could. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so just reading or watching videos felt really passive to me. I'd often find myself fidgeting or getting distracted, then feeling anxious about not focusing properly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I'd go days without studying, then panic when something was due and cram everything in. I don't have reliable internet at home, so I had to plan around when I could get access somewhere else. That made it really hard to stick to any kind of regular schedule, and the unpredictability stressed me out even though I tried to stay calm about it.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or just this nagging anxiety that I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something I'd missed, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The anxiety would build up until I couldn't ignore it anymore, so I'd finally make myself log in and see what needed to be done.
Q4: For a while, I got into a rhythm of logging in whenever I managed to get to a place with internet. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone. But that routine would break whenever life got complicated or I couldn't get access for a few days. When I did the mindfulness exercises they suggested, it actually helped me approach logging in with less dread.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful because I could watch them multiple times if something didn't click the first time. I watched a lot of video content because I could pause and try to work through problems myself, which fits how I learn. The discussion boards were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense to me than the official materials.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials. They just felt so dense and I'd zone out after a few paragraphs. Without being able to physically interact with the content somehow, I couldn't retain anything. I also skipped some of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra pressure when I was already struggling with the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to the assignments or exams. If I couldn't figure out why I was doing something, my motivation just disappeared. I also valued things that let me practice and make mistakes without it counting against my grade, because the stakes of everything felt so high already.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Part of me knew they could help, but my anxiety would tell me I should focus on the required stuff first. Then I'd never get to the optional things because I was always behind on something else. Required activities at least had a deadline that forced me to engage, even if I was stressed about it.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download videos and materials when I had internet access, then study them offline at home. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and took handwritten notes because writing things out helps me remember better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen, just me with paper and pen trying to work through problems.
Q10: Most of my real studying probably looked invisible to the system. I'd watch a video once online, then replay it in my head while practicing problems on paper at home. I'd also study with a friend sometimes, talking through concepts out loud, which really helped me understand things but wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once to download it, then spend hours with it offline. The LMS would just show one quick login, but I might have studied that material for an entire afternoon. It was the only way I could manage with my internet situation, so I got pretty good at grabbing everything I needed in one session.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety spiked and I started avoiding the course because just thinking about how behind I was made me feel sick. I also had some family stuff happening that took up my mental energy. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me get through that period without completely shutting down.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities made it hard to find time, and when I did have time, I was often too tired to engage properly. The confusion from missing classes made everything take longer too, which created this frustrating cycle.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd finish and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. It was like my body was going through the motions but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time, which just added to my anxiety.
Q15: I avoided the exam review materials because they made my anxiety worse. I knew they were important, but looking at them reminded me of how much I didn't understand. I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I felt more prepared, but that moment never really came. It's a cycle I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due, working frantically to get it done. The rest of the time I'd be aware of upcoming deadlines but somehow couldn't make myself start early. My assignment scores were actually decent because I'd hyperfocus when the pressure was on.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep trying. It reminded me I wasn't completely hopeless. But when feedback was critical or I got a bad grade, I'd spiral and avoid the course for a while. The mindfulness practice helped me handle negative feedback better toward the end of the semester.
Q18: The structure was overwhelming with 17 courses to keep track of. I felt like I was constantly switching between different requirements and couldn't go deep into anything. The course itself had a lot of content released at once, which made it hard for me to pace myself. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize.
Q19: Having videos available was great because I could learn at my own pace. But the lack of hands-on activities made it harder for me since I'm a kinesthetic learner. I wished there were more interactive elements or practical exercises. The discussion boards helped a bit, but they weren't quite the same as actually doing something.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was really active in the LMS, watching videos, checking discussions, but my exam score was terrible. For me, it was anxiety making it hard to perform under pressure, plus the disconnect between how I learn best and how I was being tested. Activity doesn't equal understanding, especially when you're struggling.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone has good resources outside the LMS or learns differently. Some people can read a textbook once and get it. That's not me though. I needed to engage a lot with the materials, but even then, something wasn't translating to my exam performance. Maybe they just have better test-taking skills.
Q22: I think logs can show patterns, like when someone is active and how often they engage with different materials. But they miss the quality of that engagement and what's happening in someone's head. They can't tell if you're anxious, confused, or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture offline studying or the emotional experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to learn more, when you think about the content even outside of study time. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to the course somehow, like it matters to my life. I wanted to feel that way, but anxiety kept getting in the way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. It also wouldn't capture that I genuinely wanted to do well but something kept blocking me from performing on exams. My effort was real, even if my grades don't reflect it.
